resources.string: Re-fetch and recalculate your full history within your retention period. Runs in the background — you can keep using the app.
Progress while recalculating your full Health Connect history.
Health Connect is not installed on this device.\nPlease install it from the Play Store.
The percentage of time actually asleep while in bed.

Your 7-day rolling heart health score.
Based on how often and how hard you challenge your heart.

• 100+: Optimal
• 75-99: Neutral
• 50-74: Warning
• < 50: Poor

Preparation for stress based on recent load & recovery.

• 85–100: Peak
• 60–84: Maintain
• 30–59: Caution
• < 30: High Fatigue

Total quality of rest based on duration and cycles.

• 80–100: Optimal
• 60–79: Fair
• < 60: Poor

Measures how regular your sleep schedule is.

High consistency stabilizes your internal clock, improving deep sleep and energy levels.

• ≥ 80%%: Optimal
• 60–79%%: Neutral
• 40–59%%: Warning
• < 40%%: Poor

Consistency Window: ±%1$d min grace period before score drops.
Average heart rate during sleep.\nTarget: lower or equal to your 30-day rolling average. (Lower = Recovered)
Variation between heartbeats in milliseconds.\nTarget: Within or above your 30-day rolling average. (Higher = Recovered)
Resting heart rate calculated as the low-percentile of the entire sleep period.\n\nBaseline: %1$d bpm %2$s (%3$d bpm)
Resting heart rate calculated as the low-percentile of the entire sleep period.\n\nNot enough data to calculate baseline.

The ACWR (Acute:Chronic Workload Ratio). Compares recent training load to your baseline.

• 0.8–1.3: In your normal range — sweet spot
• 1.3–1.5: Load increasing; linear decay begins
• 1.5–2.0: Overtraining; high injury risk
• >2.0: Severe overtraining; recovery needed
• 0.5–0.8: Under-training
• <0.5: Severe under-training
We found data from multiple sources. Pick the device whose readings should drive your scores — we\'ll fall back to other devices for days it didn\'t record.
No specific devices were detected. We\'ll use all available data sources.
Which device should we use for health metrics?
No devices found from Health Connect
Pick the source device individually for each health data type. "All devices" (the default) applies no source filter.
A drop of 18+ bpm in the first minute is considered good.
A 100-point summary of last night\'s sleep, made of three parts:
**Duration (50%)** — how much sleep you got, compared to your goal (default 8 hours, configurable). Includes a small adjustment for how efficient your time in bed was.

**Architecture (25%)** — how much of your sleep was deep (slow-wave) sleep and REM. Both matter. Deep sleep is when most physical recovery happens; REM is when your brain processes memory and emotion. Targets are age-specific to account for the natural biological decline in deep sleep across the lifespan (Ohayon 2004).

**Restoration (25%)** — how rested your estimated recovery-related physiology looks. We use the natural log of RMSSD (**lnRMSSD**) and overnight resting heart rate (**RHR**) to compute Z-scores. The log transformation is the scientific gold standard (Plews 2013, Buchheit 2014) for monitoring recovery, as it normalises the skewed distribution of raw HRV data.
**85-100** Excellent. You slept enough, your sleep stages looked balanced, and your autonomic recovery markers were strong.
**70-84** Good. Most components are healthy; one is slightly below your norm.
**50-69** Fair. Likely a duration shortfall, fragmented sleep, or an off night for HRV/RHR.
**Below 50** Poor. Multiple components are below typical. One bad night is rarely meaningful; a streak deserves attention.
*Deep sleep target 15-25%* of total sleep, with the healthy range narrowing as you age. We do not penalize you if your wearable reports unusual numbers — wearable stage detection is imperfect.
*REM target 20-25%* of total sleep, also drifting slightly downward with age.
*Sleep efficiency above 85%* is considered good (American Academy of Sleep Medicine consensus).

This score asks: **do you go to bed and wake up at roughly the same times each day?** Schedule regularity is independently linked to better metabolic, cognitive, and cardiovascular outcomes — sometimes more strongly than sleep duration itself (Windred et al. 2023, UK Biobank).

We compare each night\'s bedtime and wake time to your *typical* (median) bedtime and wake time over the last 14 days. The bigger the deviation, the lower the daily score. The number you see is a 7-day rolling average so a single late night doesn\'t tank it.
Within ±30 minutes of your typical times → full score.
More than 90 minutes off → score is 0 for that day.
You can tighten the threshold to 15 minutes if you\'re aiming for athlete-grade regularity, or relax it up to 90 minutes.

**A caveat for shift workers and biphasic sleepers.** This metric is calibrated for people with one main sleep period per day. If you work rotating shifts or sleep in two segments by choice, the score may misclassify your schedule. We exclude any single sleep period under 3 hours from the median calculation so naps don\'t pull your \"typical\" times around — but this rule has imperfect coverage of every sleep pattern.
A daily 0-100 number reflecting whether your recent training load is in a healthy range.
We compute two rolling averages of your training load:
The ratio (ATL ÷ CTL) tells us whether you\'ve recently spiked above your recent norm. Around 1.0 means you\'re training in line with your fitness; substantially above 1.0 means a relative spike.
*Peak (85-100)* — your recent load is consistent with your fitness.
*Caution (30-59)* — meaningful load spike; consider an easier day.
*High Fatigue (<30)* — large spike vs. your norm.
**What we don\'t do.** We don\'t penalize you for resting. A week of light activity will *not* drop Readiness; the score is designed for load *spikes*, not undertraining.
## What the app needs from you
We read from Android Health Connect:
**Sleep sessions** (with stages if your device records them)
**Heart rate** during exercise sessions (for training load)
The app reads only — it never writes. You can revoke access at any time in Health Connect settings.
If a particular metric is missing on a given day, we\'ll either:
show the score with a \"data partial\" badge and explain which component was estimated, or
skip the score for that day entirely if too much is missing (especially total sleep time).
## How long until your scores stabilise
Biological baselines take time to learn. We are explicit about the phases:

**Days 1-7 (Calibration).** We collect data. Sleep Score and Circadian Consistency are visible from the first night you log enough sleep, but **HRV-based Restoration is hidden** because a single night of HRV is not interpretable on its own. Readiness uses a placeholder fitness value based on the activity level you tell us during onboarding.

**Days 8-21 (Provisional).** We start showing all three scores, but Restoration uses a population-typical estimate of how much your HRV varies day to day. Expect more variability than the mature score.
**Days 22-60 (Maturing).** Your personal HRV mean is settled, and we begin blending your personal day-to-day variability with the population estimate. Confidence indicator visible.
**Day 60+ (Mature).** All scores use your own personal baselines for both the average and the variability. This is when small day-to-day differences become trustworthy.
If you wear your tracker only 3-5 nights a week, the timeline lengthens proportionally.
**HRV (Heart Rate Variability)** — the millisecond-level variation in time between heartbeats. Higher generally indicates better autonomic recovery, *up to a point*.

**RMSSD** — the specific HRV measure most apps use. We work with the natural log of RMSSD (**lnRMSSD**) internally because it linearizes the naturally skewed distribution of heart rate variability, making statistical comparison (Z-scores) valid (Plews 2013, Buchheit 2014).
**RHR (Resting Heart Rate)** — your nocturnal heart rate, averaged across deep portions of sleep.
**Deep sleep / Slow-Wave Sleep / N3** — the deepest stage of NREM sleep; growth-hormone release is concentrated here.
**REM** — the dreaming stage, important for memory and emotional processing.
**TRIMP (Training Impulse)** — a single number summarizing the intensity-weighted duration of an exercise session.
**ATL / CTL** — short-term and long-term rolling averages of TRIMP. Borrowed from Banister\'s training-load model and used by most cycling and running apps.

**1. Wearable stage detection is imperfect.** Even premium devices misclassify deep and REM sleep on individual nights. We use age-adjusted targets and don\'t penalize you for differences within ~5% of the target. Architecture differences within that margin are not meaningful.

**2. Population norms are not destiny.** The age-banded deep sleep ranges come from polysomnography studies of healthy adults. Your healthy normal may sit anywhere in your age band. Our scoring uses age-banded targets so the ceiling shifts with you.

**3. The ACWR (Readiness load ratio) is descriptive, not predictive.** The methodological literature (Lolli et al. 2019; Impellizzeri et al. 2020, 2021) has demonstrated that the acute-to-chronic ratio is often a mathematical artifact and is not a validated *causal* injury predictor. We follow the Gabbett (2016) quadratic penalty model but present it strictly as a **load change indicator** to help you visualize spikes in training intensity, not as a diagnostic injury risk score.

**4. Recovery flag.** When your HRV and RHR patterns suggest possible physiological stress (such as early illness or functional overreaching), we surface a soft notification. To ensure accuracy and filter out acute noise, the algorithm requires the thresholds to be breached on **two consecutive nights** (Mishra 2020, Le Meur 2013). This is informational only, not medical advice.
**5. One night is noise; trends are signal.** Treat any single day\'s score as a data point, not a verdict. Look at the 7-day trend.
**6. This app does not diagnose anything.** If you suspect sleep apnea, a heart condition, an infection, or an injury, see a clinician.

*Selected primary sources informing the scoring: Buysse 1989 (PSQI); Buysse 2014 (RU-SATED); Ohayon et al. 2004, 2017; Hirshkowitz et al. 2015 (NSF); Boulos et al. 2019 (Lancet Respir Med); Plews et al. 2012, 2013, 2014 (HRV monitoring); Buchheit 2014 (Front Physiol); Le Meur et al. 2013 (parasympathetic hyperactivity); Mishra et al. 2020 (Nat Biomed Eng); Quer et al. 2021 (Nat Med); Phillips et al. 2017 (Sleep Regularity Index); Lunsford-Avery et al. 2018; Windred et al. 2023/2024; Khalsa et al. 2003 (phase-response curve); Banister 1991; Foster 1998; Gabbett 2016; Lolli et al. 2019; Impellizzeri et al. 2020/2021.*

This app describes a wellness-oriented monitoring framework derived from consumer wearable signals and sports-science literature. The framework is **not validated for medical diagnosis, disease screening, treatment guidance, or injury prediction**. Physiological metrics such as HRV, sleep staging, and resting heart rate are non-specific and can be influenced by numerous behavioral, environmental, pharmacological, and measurement-related factors.
If you have concerns about your health, sleep, or recovery, consult a qualified healthcare provider.

Average sleeping oxygen saturation last night.

• Optimal: 98–100%
• Normal: 95–97%
• Warning: 90–94%
• Poor: <90%
Backups are stored locally on your device. You can create a new backup at any time or restore from a previous one.
This password will be used to encrypt your backups with AES-256. If you lose it, you won\'t be able to restore your data.
This will replace all local health data with the contents of %1$s and restart the app. This cannot be undone.
The secure encryption key for your database could not be decrypted. This typically happens if the Android Keystore was cleared or corrupted.
Database successfully restored! Please restart the application.
Failed to restore backup. Please ensure you selected the correct file.
If you have a previously exported backup file (.zip), you can select it to restore your data safely.
If you don\'t have a backup, you can reset the database. This will permanently delete all existing local health dashboards and records.
Used for BMI calculation in weight tracking
Your personal recovery & readiness tracker — powered entirely by your own data.
Weighted by duration, architecture, and restoration quality.
Personal baselines calculated from your last 30 days of data.
Acute vs. chronic workload ratio to guide your recovery.
All data stays on your device. Nothing is ever uploaded to any server.
Your birthday, gender, and activity profile are used to calculate your max heart rate and personalize scores. They are stored only on your device.
Use colors derived from your wallpaper (Android 12+)
This app needs access to Sleep, Heart Rate, HRV, and Exercise data from Health Connect to calculate your scores.
Zones are used for TRIMP and workout intensity tracking.
Invalid: All values must be 1–%1$d bpm and strictly increasing
Calibrating — averages shown may improve as more nights are recorded.
Today\'s heart rate range across all activities. Tap to see full timeline and zone breakdown.

Body Mass Index (Normal: 18.5–24.9)

Under 25: Normal
25-30: Overweight
30+: Obese
Failed to update threshold settings. Changes rolled back.
Automatically delete data older than the retention period.
Clear all data from Health Connect and reload the last 60 days.
Overrides the 30-day rolling median used in scoring.
Use the Nth percentile lowest heart rate reading of the entire sleep period (e.g., 5%% = lowest 5%% threshold). Lower values = lower RHR estimate. Default: 5%%.
Adjusts how quickly you earn PAI points. Default: 0.20.
HRV ratio to baseline to be considered Optimal (e.g. 100-120%%).
HRV ratio to baseline to be considered Warning (e.g. 80-100%%).
RHR ratio to baseline to be considered Optimal (e.g. 80-100%%).
RHR ratio to baseline to be considered Warning (e.g. 100-120%%).
±Grace period (in minutes) around your median bedtime and wake time before your score starts to drop. Default: 30 min.
Number of recent sleep sessions scored to compute your current consistency. Default: 7.
Number of past sleep sessions used to calculate your median bedtime anchor. Default: 14.
Target steps per day. Reaching this goal shows as Optimal on the dashboard.
Circadian threshold adjustment. Range: 0 to 90 minutes
The percentage of time actually asleep while in bed.

This app reads sleep, heart rate, heart rate variability, and exercise data from Health Connect solely to compute your personal recovery and readiness scores. All data is processed locally on your device and is never uploaded to any server.
Total training impulse - measures training intensity and duration.
Average heart rate during the workout.
Total strain gained from this workout.
Personal Activity Intelligence points gained from this workout.
Body fat percentage from Health Connect
